title: Songs Through Yellow Glass
synopsis: Told from the perspective of a classroom globe, this story follows Isla through a school year aboard a scratched yellow submarine. When only she can hear mysterious songs echoing through the vessel, her unique ability becomes the key to understanding the underwater world around them.

chapters.title: The Globe Remembers September
chapters.content:
The scratched yellow submarine settled into its first day of school with a gentle creak that nobody else seemed to notice.

I am the classroom globe, and I have been watching from my corner for exactly forty-seven submarine school years. My surface shows all the oceans we might visit, though the salt water outside our fogged portholes looks nothing like my painted blue. Today, twenty-three students file through the submarine's round door, their footsteps echoing against the curved metal walls.

Isla enters last, as she always does. Her long wavy red hair catches the filtered underwater light streaming through our portholes, and her bright green eyes scan the room like she's reading invisible maps. Very fair freckled skin makes her look like she's been kissed by sea foam. She chooses the desk closest to me, which pleases me greatly.

Mrs. Nautilus begins explaining how their classroom submarine works. The air recycler hums its steady song. The depth meter ticks softly. The communication tubes whistle when messages arrive from other submarine schools. Everything makes its proper sound.

But then I notice Isla's head tilt slightly to the left. Her bright green eyes grow wide and distant, the way they do when she hears something no one else can hear. Her freckled face turns toward the starboard porthole, where blue-green water swirls with tiny particles of light.

"Did anyone else hear that?" Isla asks, raising her hand.

Mrs. Nautilus pauses mid-sentence about the submarine's periscope system. "Hear what, dear?"

"The singing. It sounds like... like whales, but different. Smaller."

Twenty-two other students look around with blank expressions. Mrs. Nautilus smiles kindly. "Perhaps it's the water recycling system. Old submarines make many noises."

But I have been in this submarine for forty-seven years, and I know every sound it makes. The water recycler gurgles. The air system whooshes. The hull creaks and pings. None of them sing.

Isla nods politely and returns to her worksheets, but her bright green eyes keep drifting toward the portholes. Her pencil hovers over a math problem about counting fish, and I can tell she's listening for something that only she can hear.

As September swirls around us like underwater currents, I begin to suspect that Isla's ears work differently than everyone else's. And in a submarine school floating through mysterious waters, this might be exactly what we need.

illustrations.description: Chalkboard style drawing of a submarine classroom interior with curved metal walls and fogged portholes. Isla with long wavy red hair, very fair freckled skin, and bright green eyes sits at a desk near a globe, tilting her head as if listening. Other students work at their desks while Mrs. Nautilus points to a diagram. White and colored chalk show the submarine's details, with equation doodles on the dark green board background and dusty chalk texture.
illustrations.alt: Interior view of submarine classroom with Isla listening intently while others work

chapters.title: December's Deep Song
chapters.content:
Winter arrives in the deep waters where we spend December, and the submarine's heating system works harder to keep us warm.

Snow cannot fall this far underwater, but something else drifts past our portholes—tiny silver fish that move like frozen stars. They school and scatter in patterns that remind me of blizzards I've seen in my atlas pictures. Isla watches them through the scratched yellow glass, her breath fogging the porthole from inside.

It happens during Silent Reading Time. Every student has their head buried in books about underwater adventures and submarine mysteries. The only sounds should be pages turning and the submarine's familiar mechanical heartbeat.

But Isla's bright green eyes snap up from her book about dolphin families. Her long wavy red hair falls across her very fair freckled skin as she turns her head toward the port side of the submarine. She's listening again.

"There it is," she whispers, so quietly that only I can hear from my nearby corner.

This time, she doesn't ask if others hear it. She's learned that the song belongs only to her ears. Instead, she pulls out a notebook and begins drawing what the sound looks like to her. Wavy lines like music, but also like water currents. Dots that could be musical notes or tiny fish or both.

Mrs. Nautilus notices Isla's drawing and drifts over to her desk. "What are you working on, dear?"

"The winter song," Isla says matter-of-factly. "It's different from the September one. Deeper. And it has more voices now."

"More voices?"

Isla nods, adding another curved line to her drawing. "Like a choir, but underwater. They're trying to tell us something, I think."

Mrs. Nautilus studies the drawing with the same expression adults wear when they're trying to understand something that doesn't fit into their regular world. "Would you like to share this with the class during Show and Tell?"

"Maybe," Isla says. "But I need to listen more first. Songs this deep take time to understand."

From my corner, I watch the silver fish outside reorganize themselves into new patterns. If I didn't know better, I'd say they were swimming in rhythm with whatever Isla is hearing. But globes aren't supposed to notice such things.

Still, I find myself wishing I had ears instead of just painted oceans.

illustrations.description: Chalkboard style drawing showing Isla with long wavy red hair, very fair freckled skin, and bright green eyes drawing wavy musical lines in her notebook. Through the fogged submarine portholes, tiny silver fish swim in formation like underwater snowflakes. The submarine's interior is warm and glowing, contrasting with the deep blue-green water outside. White chalk shows the scene details against the dark green board with equation doodles and dusty texture.
illustrations.alt: Isla drawing musical patterns while silver fish swim past submarine windows

chapters.title: March Melodies
chapters.content:
Spring in the submarine means rising toward shallower waters where more sunlight can reach us through the scratched yellow portholes.

The water outside changes from deep navy to bright turquoise, and new kinds of sea life begin appearing at our windows. Jellyfish pulse past like living umbrellas. Schools of bright orange fish dart between forests of swaying kelp. Everything moves faster here, as if the spring sunlight has given the ocean extra energy.

Isla has grown braver about her invisible songs. During Morning Meeting, she raises her hand and says, "The music is getting happier. I think we're going somewhere special."

Mrs. Nautilus has learned to listen when Isla talks about her songs. "What makes you think that?"

"The rhythm changed yesterday," Isla explains, tucking her long wavy red hair behind one ear. Her bright green eyes sparkle with excitement. "It used to be slow and deep, like sleeping. Now it's quicker, like dancing. And there are new voices—higher ones, like children singing."

Some of her classmates lean forward with interest. Marcus asks, "What do you think they're singing about?"

Isla considers this seriously. Her very fair freckled skin flushes slightly pink as she thinks. "I think they're welcoming us. Like when you sing hello to someone new."

"Who's singing?" asks Sarah.

"I'm not sure yet," Isla admits. "But they sound friendly."

That afternoon, during Science Time, something extraordinary happens. Mrs. Nautilus is explaining how whales communicate through song when Isla suddenly stands up, her eyes wide with discovery.

"They're not whales," she says with absolute certainty. "They're smaller. Much smaller. And there are thousands of them."

As if summoned by her words, the water around our submarine fills with tiny lights—bioluminescent fish no bigger than coins, pulsing in synchronized patterns. The whole class rushes to the portholes to watch them dance.

"Lanternfish," breathes Mrs. Nautilus. "I've never seen so many at once."

But I notice that Isla isn't watching the light show. She's still listening, her head tilted in that familiar way, a smile growing across her freckled face.

"Now I understand," she whispers. "They've been singing to guide us home."

illustrations.description: Chalkboard style drawing of thousands of tiny glowing lanternfish surrounding the yellow submarine, their bioluminescent lights creating patterns in the turquoise water. Inside, Isla with long wavy red hair, very fair freckled skin, and bright green eyes stands with her hand raised, smiling with understanding while her classmates crowd around the portholes. The scene is filled with movement and light, drawn in white and colored chalk on the dark green board with equation doodles and dusty texture.
illustrations.alt: Submarine surrounded by glowing lanternfish with Isla having a moment of realization

chapters.title: June's Journey Home
chapters.content:
By the end of the school year, our submarine has surfaced in a lagoon where the water is so clear you can see straight to the sandy bottom.

Palm trees lean over the edges of our temporary harbor, and tropical birds call to each other in the warm air that now flows through our opened hatches. This is where submarine schools come for summer break—a secret place that appears on no regular maps, only on globes like me who have learned to see with more than painted eyes.

Isla sits by the largest porthole during our Final Day celebration. Her long wavy red hair is braided with small shells her classmates found on yesterday's swimming expedition. Her bright green eyes reflect the dancing light from the water, and her very fair freckled skin has gained a few new freckles from the island sun.

"The song is complete now," she tells me quietly, knowing somehow that I have been listening to her listening all year long.

I want to ask her what she means, but globes cannot speak out loud. We can only turn slowly on our stands and hope our painted continents convey our curiosity.

Isla seems to understand anyway. "It wasn't just one song," she explains. "It was like... like a map made of music. The September song told us we were starting a journey. The December song helped us navigate the deep waters safely. The March song guided us up toward the light. And now..."

She presses her palm against the porthole glass, and I swear the lanternfish outside pulse brighter in response.

"Now the June song is saying thank you. And welcome home. And see you next year."

Mrs. Nautilus appears beside Isla's desk. "What will you remember most about our submarine school year?"

Isla thinks for a moment, watching the lanternfish spiral in complicated patterns just beyond the scratched yellow glass. "I'll remember that being different isn't scary. It's useful. My ears work differently than everyone else's, but that meant I could hear the songs that kept us safe."

"And what will you do with that knowledge?"

"Listen," Isla says simply. "There are probably lots of songs in the world that only some people can hear. Maybe if we all pay attention to our different kinds of listening, we'll understand more about where we're supposed to go."

As the afternoon sun filters down through the lagoon water, I realize that I have learned something too. For forty-seven years, I thought my job was to show students where places are. But Isla has taught me that sometimes the most important destinations can only be found by hearing their songs.

And as our submarine prepares to sleep through the summer in this magical harbor, I find myself already looking forward to September, when new students will arrive and new songs will begin. But I will especially look forward to seeing Isla again, ready for another year of listening to the music that others cannot hear.

Because every submarine school needs at least one student who can hear where the currents want to take us home.

illustrations.description: Chalkboard style drawing of the submarine surfaced in a crystal-clear tropical lagoon surrounded by palm trees. Isla with long wavy red hair braided with shells, very fair freckled skin, and bright green eyes sits by a porthole with her palm pressed against the glass. Lanternfish glow in spiraling patterns in the water around the submarine. Warm sunlight filters down through the clear water, and the scene has a magical, peaceful quality. White and colored chalk capture the tropical setting on the dark green board with equation doodles and dusty texture.
illustrations.alt: Submarine in tropical lagoon with Isla touching the porthole as lanternfish dance outside

coverPrompt.description: Chalkboard style drawing showing a scratched yellow submarine with fogged portholes floating underwater. Inside one porthole, we glimpse Isla with long wavy red hair, very fair freckled skin, and bright green eyes, listening intently. Musical notes drawn in white chalk float around the submarine. School desks and a globe are visible inside. The dark green chalkboard background shows faint equation doodles and dusty chalk texture.
coverPrompt.alt: Cover illustration of a yellow submarine classroom underwater with Isla visible through a porthole

questions.question: Who tells this story?
questions.explanation: The story is narrated by the classroom globe, who has been watching the submarine school for forty-seven years.

questions.question: What makes Isla different from her classmates?
choices: She can hear songs no one else hears
questions.explanation: Isla can hear underwater songs that her classmates and teacher cannot hear, making her listening abilities unique.

questions.question: How does the song change from September to March?
choices: It goes from slow and deep to quicker and happier
questions.explanation: Isla notices the song changes from slow and deep like sleeping to quicker like dancing, with new higher voices added.

questions.question: What creatures are actually making the songs Isla hears?
questions.explanation: Isla realizes the songs come from thousands of tiny lanternfish, not whales as originally thought.

questions.question: Where does the submarine end up in June?
choices: At the bottom of the ocean
questions.explanation: The submarine surfaces in a clear tropical lagoon with palm trees, where submarine schools go for summer break.

questions.question: What does Isla learn about being different?
questions.explanation: Isla learns that her different way of hearing isn't scary but useful, as it helped keep the submarine safe by following the guiding songs.

questions.question: How long has the globe been in the submarine school?
questions.explanation: The globe narrator mentions having watched the submarine school for exactly forty-seven years.
